problem_summary: 2014 MCM Problem C (ICM, interdisciplinary network science). Teams were given the Erdős coauthor network and a set of 16 foundational network-science papers (NetSciFoundation.pdf), and asked to (a) build influence-measurement models to find the most influential coauthors and the most influential papers, (b) discuss the science of networks and how influence/centrality differs across network types, and (c) generalize the methodology to a second domain. This O-award team (Control #27688) built two distinct influence models — one for the undirected coauthor network and one for the directed citation network — then transferred the citation model to a ~500-node US media corporate-ownership network as the third application, validating each ranking against external real-world data.
models_used: Coauthor influence model (undirected): a two-axis ranking that deliberately rejects path-based centralities. Defines Authority C_a(i) = n_Ei / (mean coauthoring count with Erdős), i.e. normalized count of co-authored papers with Erdős, and Importance = normalized Eigenvector Centrality C_e. Procedure: filter to researchers with C_a >= 2 (52 remain), then rank survivors by eigenvector centrality.

Four classical centrality measures computed as exploratory baseline in UCINET: Degree, Eigenvector (Bonacich), Closeness, Betweenness centrality (formulas given for each), plus degree distribution and clustering coefficient as network descriptors.

Citation influence model (directed DAG): a custom food-chain / energy-transfer contribution-propagation model. Initial contribution coefficient C_i = N_cited(i) / (t_now - t_pub(i)) — citation count discounted by paper age — assigned to source (in-degree-0) nodes. Self-contribution coefficient S controls how much influence a node retains vs. passes upstream. Propagated value C'_i = C_i + sum over in-neighbors p of [C_p*(1-S)/d_out(i)].

Custom DAG propagation algorithm AL-CITATION built on topological-sort order (Kahn-style peeling of in-degree-0 vertices), distributing (1-S) fraction of each node's coefficient equally along out-edges and retaining S. Explicitly chosen OVER PageRank and HITS, argued to be O(n+e) vs. their iterative matrix-multiplication cost.

Corporate-ownership transfer model: reuses the citation DAG algorithm. Company initial value V = alpha*v_c + beta*v_s + gamma*v_re + chi*v_tp + delta*v_p + epsilon*v_r (weighted sum of cash, stock, real estate, technical personnel, patents, relationships), and control coefficient S-bar = v_s(sub)/v_s(parent) (stock-ownership proportion) plays the role of the self-contribution coefficient.

h5-index proposed as the metric for evaluating an aggregate entity (university / department / journal), argued superior to raw paper count or total citations because it jointly captures quality and quantity.
key_techniques: Network-type-driven model selection: explicitly recognizing the coauthor graph as undirected social (no transitivity, 'not an information-flow problem') vs. the citation graph as a DAG with strict partial order, and justifying DIFFERENT models for each rather than forcing one metric on both.

Deliberate simplification with stated rationale: dropping closeness and betweenness for the coauthor network because coauthorship has no 'bridge'/transitive semantics — turning a modeling omission into a defensible design choice (listed as Strength #1).
Physical analogy as a modeling device: mapping citation influence onto food-chain energy transfer (one-directional, decaying with each hop) to motivate the propagation formula intuitively.
Exploiting DAG structure for algorithmic efficiency: using topological sorting to get an exact, single-pass O(n+e) solution instead of iterative PageRank/HITS, and stating the complexity explicitly.

Sensitivity / stability analysis on the one free parameter: sweeping S over {0.5, 0.75, 0.9} and a random value in (0.5,0.95), showing the top ranking (paper 14, then 2/4) barely changes — and benchmarking against PageRank to show agreement.

External ground-truth validation at every stage: verifying top-5 coauthors via Wikipedia/CVs (Pólya Prize, Whitehead Prize, IMO results), and verifying the 15 top media companies against an independently published 2001 'top 20 media companies' list (9 of 15 appear).
Algorithm documentation in three redundant forms: prose steps, formal pseudo-code (AL-CITATION), and a flowchart — making the contribution easy for judges to verify.

Data-quality flagging as an assumption: explicitly noting paper 7's data looked wrong and treating it as an isolated vertex, and restricting the citation network to papers citing >2 of the 16 to keep scope finite.
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective this paper wins because it treats 'influence' as a modeling problem rather than a software-output problem, and backs every claim with verification. Three things stand out. (1) It demonstrates genuine network-science understanding: it does not just run UCINET centralities and report them — it shows the four centralities give inconsistent rankings (Table 3) and argues centrality alone cannot capture authority + transitive influence, which is exactly the insight the problem is probing. (2) It produces a genuine modeling artifact — a custom DAG contribution-propagation algorithm derived from a food-chain analogy, with pseudo-code, a flowchart, an O(n+e) complexity claim, and an explicit comparison to PageRank/HITS. Inventing a defensible algorithm (rather than invoking a library) is what separates O from Meritorious. (3) It closes the loop with reality at every step: top researchers cross-checked against real prizes/CVs, and the corporate model validated against an independently published media-company ranking (9 of 15 matched). The sensitivity sweep over S plus the PageRank cross-check shows the result is robust, not cherry-picked. The third (corporate-ownership) application is not a throwaway — it reuses the SAME algorithm with a clean mapping (company value <-> initial contribution, stock-ownership share <-> self-contribution S), proving generality, which is the heart of an ICM/interdisciplinary problem."
section_highlights: "Structure mirrors the problem and signals competence. The summary sheet states all three answers up front with named winners (top-5 researchers, papers 14/4/2, 15 companies) — judges see the payoff in 30 seconds. Section 3 (coauthor) and Section 4 (citation) each follow build-model -> define-metrics -> compute -> validate, and Section 4.2 inserts an explicit 'Compare the coauthor and citation network' subsection that motivates why a new model is needed (transitivity, DAG, publication-time effects) — this is the strongest writing move in the paper because it justifies the central design decision before presenting it. The algorithm is given three ways (prose Steps, pseudo-code AL-CITATION, flowchart Figure 6). A dedicated Stability Test subsection (4.3.3) with the S-sweep table and PageRank column preempts the obvious 'how did you pick S=0.75?' question. Strengths/Weaknesses honestly admits the algorithm only works for DAGs and that the company value model omits factors. The applications section (SNS information spread, acquisition decisions citing the real Lenovo-IBM PC deal, military 'decapitation strike') ties the abstract metrics to concrete decisions. Real references (Newman's 'Networks: An Introduction', Bonacich, Kleinberg HITS, Page/Brin PageRank) signal the team read primary sources."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Authority is conflated with co-authoring count with Erdős specifically, which is an artifact of this exact dataset (everyone has Erdős number 1) and does not generalize to a real coauthor network — the team even assumes 'more times coauthored with Erdős => more authority,' a strong unjustified leap.

The food-chain analogy is intuitive but not derived: the propagation formula C'_i = C_i + sum C_p(1-S)/d_out(i) and the choice to divide by OUT-degree of the citing paper are asserted, not justified from a model of how influence actually flows; units of the initial coefficient (citations per year) are never reconciled with the dimensionless propagation.

S = 0.75 is picked arbitrarily ('we have no way to measure each paper's self-contribution, hence we regard it as 0.75'); the stability test mitigates but does not eliminate this — a single global S for all papers/companies is a strong homogeneity assumption.

The O(n+e) efficiency argument is largely irrelevant at the actual problem scale (16 papers, 511 nodes); the claimed advantage over PageRank only matters for large graphs the paper never actually runs, so the 'design a faster algorithm' motivation is somewhat manufactured.

Corporate value weights (alpha..epsilon) are defined symbolically but never assigned values — the company model sets 'the same initial value of companies' because data was incomplete, so the headline V formula is essentially decorative and the ownership ranking is driven almost entirely by graph structure, not the value model.

Numerous English/typographical errors (UCInet vs UCINET, 'eihenvector', 'filed', 'case' for 'cash', 'nest step') — tolerated in an O paper because the modeling is strong, but a risk in closer competition.

Validation is informal: 'top researchers really make much contribution' from Wikipedia bios is qualitative confirmation bias rather than a quantitative accuracy measure; '9 of 15 in top 20' is reported as success without a baseline for what random overlap would give.

Citation counts and the citation graph among the 16 papers were hand-assembled from Web of Science / Google Scholar with thresholds (cite >2 of the 16), introducing subjective network-boundary choices that strongly shape which papers rank highest.
reusable_patterns: Match the model to the graph type, and SAY SO: classify the network (undirected social vs. directed DAG vs. weighted ownership) and let that classification dictate which centralities/algorithms are valid — then write an explicit comparison subsection justifying a new model before introducing it.

Turn a deliberate omission into a stated strength: if a standard metric (e.g., betweenness) is semantically meaningless for your network, drop it and explain why in the Strengths section instead of silently ignoring it.

Two-axis filter-then-rank: when 'importance' has two incommensurable components (authority vs. connectivity), threshold on one axis to form a qualified pool, then rank within the pool by the other — cleaner than inventing an arbitrary weighted sum.

Exploit special graph structure for an exact, cheap algorithm: if the graph is a DAG, use topological-sort propagation for an O(n+e) single-pass solution and explicitly contrast it with iterative methods (PageRank/HITS) to show you understand the alternatives.
Use a physical/biological analogy (energy decaying along a food chain) to motivate a propagation rule the reader can grasp instantly — analogies make custom algorithms feel principled.

Always include a one-parameter sensitivity sweep + a benchmark against a known method: vary your single free knob over several values (and a random draw), tabulate that the top ranking is stable, and add a PageRank column to show agreement. This single move answers 'is it robust?' and 'why not just use the standard method?' at once.

Validate every ranking against external real-world ground truth (prizes, CVs, an independently published industry ranking) — and report the overlap count explicitly; judges reward closing the loop with reality.

Prove generality by reusing the identical algorithm on a second domain with a clean variable mapping (citation initial-value <-> company value; self-contribution S <-> stock-ownership proportion) rather than building a brand-new model for application 3.
Document any non-trivial algorithm in three redundant forms — prose steps, pseudo-code with a named procedure, and a flowchart — so a time-pressed judge can verify it without reverse-engineering.

Flag bad input data as an explicit assumption (e.g., 'paper 7's data looks wrong, treat as isolated vertex') and state network-boundary cutoffs — this signals careful data hygiene rather than hidden hacks.
